channel.title: Finance on MinWeb
channel.description: Recent content in Finance on MinWeb

item.title: Why the Fed Won’t Cut Rates Despite Trump’s Demands
item.description:
The Federal Reserve is holding interest rates steady because the price of gas and food is surging, and no amount of interest rate cuts will fix a broken supply chain. Do not let the political shouting match distract you from the fact that this is a physical shortage, not a monetary one.

The current standoff

On March 18, 2026, President Trump demanded an immediate rate cut via social media, asking why Jerome Powell continues to keep rates high. He argues that high rates are strangling the economy.

item.title: Trump Demands Rate Cuts: Why the Fed Is Paralyzed
item.description:
The standoff between the White House and the Federal Reserve is not just a political spat; it is a signal that our economy is trapped between a rock and a hard place. No amount of interest rate maneuvering can fix a supply chain that is physically broken.

_IMG_PLACEHOLDER

President Trump wants immediate rate cuts to manage the $39 trillion national debt and jumpstart a cooling job market. Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve is holding rates steady at 3.5%–3.75%. They are paralyzed because the problem is not that money is too expensive. The problem is that energy and food are physically scarce due to global conflict.

item.title: Why Trump’s Demand for Rate Cuts Won’t Fix Stagflation
item.description:
The President wants lower interest rates, but the Federal Reserve is going to ignore him. Despite the shouting matches in the news, the White House has trapped itself. They are pushing for lower rates to save the economy, but their own geopolitical decisions are the very reason the economy is stalling.

We are stuck in a Stagflation Trap. Prices are high, jobs are shaky, and while it is easy to blame the people at the Fed, they are currently helpless against the forces actually driving your costs up.

item.title: Southwest Overhauls: Success Fuels Change
item.description:
Southwest Airlines Is Making Massive Changes (And Yes, That Includes Assigned Seats)

Southwest Airlines is going through an identity crisis.

Forget what you knew about America’s most beloved carrier - those open seats, the chaotic boarding scrum, the simple everything. It’s all getting shaken up. You’ve probably noticed the headlines about assigned seating, or maybe even experienced their glitchy flight credits system. So what’s really going on with the airline famous for bucking industry trends? Are they selling out? Smartening up? Let’s look at the facts to figure out what this means for your next flight.

item.title: Southwest's Biggest Change in 53 Years
item.description:
Southwest Airlines Is Making Huge Changes — Here’s What You Need to Know

Surprised by Southwest Airlines laying off employees for the first time ever?

You’re not alone. The airline built its reputation on friendly service, two free bags, and that unique open seating policy we all have opinions about. Now everything’s changing. Fast. Here’s what’s happening at Southwest, why it matters for your travel plans, and where this iconic airline is headed next.

item.title: Costco vs Walmart: Which Is Actually Cheaper?
item.description:
Costco vs Walmart: Which Actually Saves You More Money?

We’ve all been there - standing in the grocery aisle, calculator app open, trying to figure out if that giant bulk package at Costco is actually cheaper than the regular-sized version at Walmart.

It’s exhausting! With prices climbing 25.5% between 2020 and 2024, according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, every dollar counts more than ever.
